title: PHP-FPM Request Duration P95/P99 from Access Log
dataSources: Tail latency from FPM access log isolates application slowness independent of status snapshots.
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder; optional Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (`Splunk_TA_nix`); custom `props.conf`/`transforms.conf` for `phpfpm:*` sourcetypes
spl:
index=web sourcetype="phpfpm:access"
| eval dur_ms=tonumber(coalesce(request_time, request_duration, duration_ms))
| where isnotnull(dur_ms)
| timechart span=5m perc95(dur_ms) as p95_ms perc99(dur_ms) as p99_ms by pool
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` perc95(Web.duration) as p95_ms avg(Web.duration) as avg_ms
  from datamodel=Web.Web
  by Web.dest Web.uri_path span=5m
| where p95_ms > 2000
dataModelAcceleration: Web: enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Web` dataset once your HTTP access or proxy access logs are tagged and mapped to the model; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows. Most teams start with the raw-log search in Step 2 and add acceleration after CIM field aliases validate.
description: Percentiles from the FPM access log quantify user-visible latency better than averages alone. P95/P99 spikes often precede saturation on status metrics.
value: Supports SLO dashboards and error-budget discussions tied to real request timing.
grandmaExplanation: We use this to support service goals dashboards and error-budget discussions tied to real request timing.
implementation: Configure `access.log` with `%d` duration; ingest as `phpfpm:access`. Map `request_time` in `props.conf`.
visualization: Time series (pool utilization, queue, latency percentiles), single value alerts, top charts for scripts and methods.
knownFalsePositives: Response time spikes during JVM garbage collection, connection pool exhaustion, or backend dependency degradation. Load tests, campaigns, and cold caches also move percentiles.
